questions.question: In DNS redirection, what does the CDN authoritative name server inspect to choose which edge node IP to return?
options: The HTTP Host header sent by the browser
The IP address of the client's local DNS resolver making the query
The TCP destination port of the request
The geographic coordinates embedded in the DNS query
questions.explanation: The CDN name server looks at the source IP of the resolver asking the question, maps that IP prefix to a network region via its edge map, and returns the IP of the edge node nearest that region. A Sydney resolver gets the Sydney node.

questions.question: A CDN's nearest edge node for a region is overloaded. What does a load-aware redirection policy do, and why?
options: It queues the client's request at the nearest node until capacity frees up
It returns the origin server IP so the client bypasses the CDN
It spills the client to a slightly farther but lightly loaded edge node
It increases the DNS TTL so fewer redirection decisions are needed
questions.explanation: If the nearest node is overloaded it will deliver slow responses, recreating the problem the CDN was built to avoid. A load-aware policy maps the client to the next nearest healthy node, trading a little distance for available capacity.

questions.question: Why does the source say proxy caches alone cannot realize a CDN distribution tree?
options: Proxies are too fast and starve the origin server
Clients in one region belong to different organizations using different proxies, only one CDN can be a client's single proxy, and clients configure their own proxies
Proxies cannot cache HTTP responses at all
Proxies only work for UDP traffic, not TCP
questions.explanation: Proxy caches are configured by clients, are not shared across organizations, a client uses only one proxy so it cannot use multiple CDNs, and the CDN cannot control the configuration. DNS redirection sidesteps all three problems by putting the decision in a server the CDN runs.

questions.question: A client's DNS resolver caches the answer for www.cdn.com with a TTL of 60 seconds. Five seconds later the assigned edge node fails. What happens to that client, and what would a fresh query do instead?
options: The client is immediately redirected to a new node because the CDN pushes updates
The client keeps using the cached (now dead) node IP until the TTL expires; a fresh uncached query would be redirected to a healthy node
The client's browser automatically switches to the origin server
The DNS resolver retransmits the query every second until the node recovers
questions.explanation: DNS caching pins the client to the returned IP for the TTL. Until it expires, the client keeps connecting to the dead node and sees failures. A fresh query (from a resolver without the cached answer) would get a healthy node, which is why short TTLs are used for failover agility.
